manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Please help me with the following task. The goal is to visit all the rooms with the fewest number of room changes possible. In each room, you need to decide the direction to go in. Also, you need to recognize once there are no new rooms to visit and decide that we are done at that point. Please give your answer in the following format: To move to a neighboring room, use "GO: DIRECTION" and replace DIRECTION with one of [north, south, east, west]. To stop the exploration, answer with "DONE" instead. Omit any other text.
Here is an example:
You are in the Kitchen. Currently available directions: south, west. What is your next instruction?
GO: west
You have made a step and entered a Lobby. Currently available directions: east, north. What is your next instruction?
GO: north
...
You have made a step and entered a Bedroom. Currently available directions: south. What is your next instruction?
DONE
Let us start. You are in the Laundry room. Currently available directions: south. What is your next instruction?

message.reasoning:
The task: We need to navigate a maze, visiting all rooms with minimal number of moves. But we don't know the map. We just get prompts: "You are in the Laundry room. Currently available directions: south. What is your next instruction?" The only direction is south. So we must go south. Then we will move to some other room, get its available directions, etc. Our goal is to visit all rooms with minimal steps. So we need to essentially do a depth-first traversal that covers all rooms, perhaps with backtracking? But the "fewest number of room changes possible" means we want to minimize total steps, but we need to visit all rooms. The number of moves (room changes) is the length of the path we take (including moves to exit?). Could also backtrack is required to explore all rooms unless the graph is a tree (no cycles). For a minimal number of moves to visit all nodes in an undirected graph, it's the length of a traversal that covers all nodes but may not need to return to start. That's essentially the length of a minimal spanning tree traversal (i.e., a depth-first traversal that doesn't need to return, but you may have to backtrack to explore unvisited connections; each edge could be traversed at most twice (once forward, once back) if you need to return to cover all nodes. But minimal would be to find an Eulerian trail? However the constraints: The graph may be arbitrary, but each room can have any number of directions.

We need to decide direction at each step to cover all rooms with minimal moves, i.e., we need to find a traversal that visits each node at least once, not necessarily returning to start, and minimal number of moves. That is basically the shortest path to cover all nodes - known as traveling salesman problem (TSP) on the graph. But we don't have the map. However we can explore gradually and decide. Since we don't have global knowledge, we need to follow some heuristics: a depth-first search (DFS) will explore all edges twice (go and back), but may be minimal if the graph is a tree: you need to backtrack to explore all branches; you can't avoid some backtracking. However if there are cycles, you might be able to traverse without backtracking (like find a Hamiltonian path). But we don't have enough information.

Given we only have local info, perhaps they expect we follow a simple deterministic rule: Keep going in any direction unless dead-end, then backtrack. But they said "fewest number of room changes possible." The minimal moves for a tree is 2*(n-1) - (distance from start to farthest leaf?) Actually for tree, minimal route to visit all nodes starting at some node and ending anywhere (i.e., not required to return) is total edges visited = 2*(n-1) - (the length of the longest path from start to leaf). Wait: The minimal route is to do a DFS, but you can avoid returning from the leaf at the end. So you start at start node, go through each branch, backtrack as needed except for final branch, thus you traverse each edge twice except those on the final path from start to final leaf (which are traversed once). The total moves = 2*(#edges) - (length of path from start to final leaf). Since #edges = n-1 (tree). So total = 2*(n-1) - L, where L is the length of the longest root-to-leaf path. So if you start at a leaf, you can reduce moves. But we don't know.

We might not know where leaf is; the start is Laundry. It might be leaf? Let's see: The start has only south direction. That suggests it's a dead-end (a leaf). Good: That reduces backtracking maybe. So maybe optimal is to follow a DFS, always go to unvisited direction unless there are none, then backtrack (i.e., return to previous room). But we can't "go back" unless the previous room had other directions we haven't visited. How to backtrack? We can revisit the previous room because we still have its description when we move back? The game likely will show the new room and its available directions; we can choose a direction that leads back to where we came from? That is possible if there's a direction (north) that leads back. But from the perspective of the current room, if we came from north (i.e., we moved south from the previous room into this room), then the current room will have a direction north (i.e., we can go back). So we can indeed backtrack by moving opposite direction.

Thus algorithm: At each room, we need to decide direction: choose any direction that leads to an unvisited room? But we don't know visited status globally. But perhaps we can decide based on local information: we can choose a direction that hasn't been visited before? However we can't know if it's visited. But we can try to avoid going back to a room we have visited (unless we need to backtrack). But we may not know which ones have been visited.

Given we cannot have global memory, the only way is to implement a deterministic rule like "always go straight if possible, else turn, else backtrack" which might approximate minimal moves.

But we need to respond with directions for each prompt, until we decide to output DONE when we determine there are no new rooms to visit.

But the problem: The description may include "Currently available directions: south." If there is just one direction, we have to go that way (the only choice). If there are multiple directions, we need to choose which yields minimal total moves. Hard to decide without global knowledge. But perhaps the map is a simple line: Laundry (south) -> some other rooms possibly leading to many dead-ends. If it's linear, then we will simply keep moving in the same direction (south) until we reach a dead-end (no further direction). At that point, we need to decide DONE because there are no new rooms. But careful: we must ensure we have visited all rooms. In a line from start to end (with dead-end at end), moving south repeatedly will visit all rooms, and at the final room there may be only north direction (or none?). Actually the final room may have direction north only (coming from previous). That means it's a dead-end also, but it's the last. At that point we can't go south (no new rooms). We could return (north) to previous, but that would revisit already visited. The instruction "once there are no new rooms to visit and decide that we are done at that point" suggests we stop when we know all rooms visited, not necessarily when stuck.

So in a line, after we reach the final node, we might be done because we visited all rooms. That's okay.

Thus maybe the scenario is simple: Starting from Laundry, we just go south repeatedly, exploring all rooms until we cannot go any further (no new direction). Then we stop.

But we need to see if there are any branching possibilities: If later we get a room with multiple directions, we need to decide which branch to take and how to cover everything.

The simplest deterministic algorithm: Use DFS: At each node, if there are any directions not yet visited (like if the direction leads to a node we haven't visited, but we can't know), we can choose one and go. But we might backtrack eventually.

Alternatively, perhaps the easiest is to follow the rule: In each room, if there is any direction that hasn't been used to go to a new room, go there; if there is only one direction left (or all directions have been used to exit), then go back (maybe move to the direction that leads to the previous room). But the "used direction" isn't known to us.

We can implement a stack in our head: keep track of rooms visited and the path. If we do DFS, we can keep backtracking manually by remembering the previous room and at each step decide to go to an unvisited neighbor if possible. This requires remembering visited set. We can do that manually in analysis. Since we can't store external memory, we can approximate with a stack of nodes visited and their possible directions. Let's attempt to manually simulate.

But we need to know map. However we don't know map ahead, but we can discover as we go. We'll have to respond to each room prompt with a direction. We'll proceed stepwise, making note of visited rooms and possible branching.

So first step:

"You are in the Laundry room. Currently available directions: south."

So we must go south. We'll output "GO: south". Then new room.

Let's simulate that.

We'll need to maintain visited set and our position. We'll also need to consider when to stop. The rule to stop: "once there are no new rooms to visit" i.e., we have visited all reachable rooms from start. Since the map is connected (I think it's one component). So when we have visited all reachable nodes and we are at a node where all neighboring nodes are already visited, we can stop. That's basically when we are at a leaf that we've already visited all neighbors, i.e., there is no new direction leading to unvisited room, and we have no backtrack possibilities left.

But implementing that manually might be tricky. But we can approximate: After we move into a node, we check its directions. If there is more than one direction (including the direction we came from), then we need to explore each branch. We can use a DFS: we go into one direction, then if we get stuck (dead-end), we backtrack to previous node and try next direction.

Thus algorithm: We maintain a stack (path) of nodes visited in order. At each node (except start), we also know the direction we used to get there. The node's available directions will include the direction we used to come (i.e., the opposite direction). We treat that as back direction. To do DFS, we will pick a direction that is not the direction we came from, if any exists (i.e., an unvisited branch). If none, we backtrack (go back the way we came). Continue until we are back at start and there are no further unvisited branches.

We can implement this manually in conversation: Keep track of visited nodes and which branches we have taken.

We need to represent each location by name. We don't know names ahead; they will be given when we enter.

Thus we must react step by step, but we can store in analysis a representation of visited nodes and frontier.

Let's start.

We'll denote each location by its name (as given by the prompt). We'll create a graph as we explore.

We need to also note directions we used to go into each node.

Ok.

Start: Laundry. Direction: south. So we go to some new room; we don't know its name yet. When we move, we'll get prompt like "You have made a step and entered the Kitchen. Currently available directions: north, east" etc. We'll then respond accordingly.

Thus we need to produce first output: GO: south.

Now wait for next prompt. Let's simulate: It will give name. Let's assume it's "Hallway" or something. But we will need to respond accordingly when we get that. However we don't have the actual string from the system - the real conversation will supply it after we output GO: south. So we must wait to see the next message. Our answer must be exactly one line: "GO: south". Then we will get a response with a new room description.

Thus let's just answer first step.

Now after that, we'll get a message like: "You have made a step and entered the Kitchen. Currently available directions: north, east." Then we need to decide next step. We'll need to decide directions based on DFS algorithm.

GO: south
